originalTitle: AI buildout gives tech investors new reasons to watch bond market
summary: Tech giants are depleting cash reserves and raising debt in their ambitious data center buildouts, a dynamic that's forcing investors to watch interest rates.
content:
Artificial intelligence is giving tech investors an entirely new reason to pay attention to the Federal Reserve.

For years, megacap tech companies with hefty balance sheets have been able to shrug off rising rates, which tend to weigh more heavily on smaller, less-profitable peers.

But companies that were once cash cows are depleting reserves and leveraging debt in their ambitious data center buildouts. That's making the group much more exposed to the cost of borrowing.

"Tech investors are not as used to looking at rates," Peter Boockvar, chief investment officer of One Point BFG Wealth Partners, said in an interview. "All of a sudden tech investors need to listen to what Kevin Warsh has to say, they need to start paying attention to what the inflation stats are and how the U.S. Treasury market responds to it."

Warsh held his first press conference as Fed chairman on Wednesday. The central bank indicated the possibility of a rate hike in 2026, which sparked a sell-off in equities and an increase in rates. The 10-year yield is trading near 4.45%.

Higher rates have always had an outsized impact on smaller tech companies, as investors value them based on future profits. When yields spike and the so-called "risk-free rate" rises, investors discount future cash flows, making them worth less today.

The effect of rising rates is now moving upstream. That's because tech's hyperscalers are engaged in a high-speed race to build out AI infrastructure, with Amazon , Alphabet , Microsoft and Meta projected to deploy a combined $750 billion this year, up more than 80% from 2025.

A big piece of that expansion is being funded by debt, which becomes a more difficult sell if rates are rising. Nvidia, Oracle , Amazon, Alphabet and Meta are turning to the debt market to the tune of tens of billions of dollars each.

OpenAI CFO Sarah Friar has pointed to an ability to leverage debt markets as one motivation to go public. Reuters reported on Thursday, citing two sources familiar with the matter, that bankers for SpaceX , which debuted on the Nasdaq last week, are preparing to ​meet investors about a bond offering of at least $20 billion.

"It's underappreciated," said Jeff Kilburg, CEO of KKM Financial, adding that there's an "insatiable demand" for AI-related funding. "Tech leadership is embracing debt. It's the perfect recipe for these AI folks who feel comfortable in what they want to borrow, and spend."

Tech giants need the money as some of them deplete their cash reserves they've spent years building up. Goldman Sachs recently noted that capex as a percentage of cash flow is at the highest level since the dot-com era. The firm also expects that capex this year will be closer to $920 billion, and says analyst estimates have been "too conservative" each of the past three years.

Amazon, which has forecast spending of roughly $200 billion this year, is widely expected to see negative free cash flow.

"Tech investors are learning what it's like to be an investor in old-economy industrial businesses that are capital intensive," Boockvar said. "Free cash flow is volatile and access to both debt and equity markets are crucial in order to finance it all."

Issuing debt can be a deliberate strategy. It can preserve liquidity for acquisitions while bringing flexibility when it comes to financing long-term buildouts.

Jay Woods, chief market strategist at Freedom Capital Markets, is assessing the debt risk based on each individual company, not the sector as a whole. Nvidia, for example, is in a strong cash position, with free cash flow jumping past $48.5 billion in the latest quarter , up from $26.1 billion a year earlier.

"They still have a deep cash bench, so I don't think it's that big of a red flag," Woods said about Nvidia. "It does give them flexibility."

-- CNBC'S Drew Troast contributed reporting.
chineseTitle: AI 的发展为科技投资者提供了关注债券市场的新理由
chineseSummary: 科技巨头正在耗尽现金储备，并在其雄心勃勃的数据中心建设中举债，这种动态迫使投资者关注利率。
chineseContent:
人工智能为科技投资者提供了关注美联储的全新理由。

多年来，拥有庞大资产负债表的大型科技公司一直能够摆脱利率上升的影响，而利率上升往往会给规模较小、利润较低的同行带来更大的压力。

但曾经是摇钱树的公司正在耗尽储备，并在雄心勃勃的数据中心扩建中利用债务。这使得该集团面临更大的借贷成本风险。

One Point BFG Wealth Partners 首席投资官 Peter Boockvar 在接受采访时表示：“科技投资者不太习惯关注利率。” “突然之间，科技投资者需要听听凯文·沃什的说法，他们需要开始关注通胀数据以及美国国债市场对此的反应。”

沃什周三举行了他作为美联储主席的首次新闻发布会。央行暗示2026年加息的可能性，引发股市抛售和利率上调。 10年期国债收益率接近4.45%。

较高的利率总是对小型科技公司产生巨大影响，因为投资者根据未来利润来评估它们。当收益率飙升和所谓的“无风险利率”上升时，投资者会对未来现金流进行贴现，从而使它们今天的价值下降。

利率上升的影响现在正在向上游转移。这是因为科技领域的超大规模企业正在展开一场建设人工智能基础设施的高速竞赛，亚马逊、Alphabet、微软和 Meta 预计今年将部署总计 7500 亿美元的资金，比 2025 年增长超过 80%。

这种扩张的很大一部分是通过债务融资的，如果利率上升，出售债务就会变得更加困难。英伟达（Nvidia）、甲骨文（Oracle）、亚马逊（Amazon）、Alphabet 和 Meta 正在转向债务市场，各自规模达数百亿美元。

OpenAI 首席财务官 Sarah Friar 指出，利用债务市场的能力是上市的动机之一。路透社周四援引两位知情人士的话报道称，上周在纳斯达克上市的 SpaceX 的银行家正准备与投资者会面，讨论发行至少 200 亿美元的债券。

KKM Financial 首席执行官杰夫·基尔伯格 (Jeff Kilburg) 表示：“它没有得到充分重视。”他补充说，人工智能相关资金的需求“永无止境”。 “技术领导力正在拥抱债务。对于那些对自己想要借钱和花钱感到放心的人工智能人员来说，这是完美的秘诀。”

科技巨头需要这笔资金，因为其中一些已经耗尽了多年来积累的现金储备。高盛最近指出，资本支出占现金流的百分比处于互联网时代以来的最高水平。该公司还预计今年的资本支出将接近 9200 亿美元，并表示分析师的预测在过去三年中每年都“过于保守”。

亚马逊预计今年的支出约为 2000 亿美元，人们普遍预计自由现金流为负。

布克瓦尔表示：“科技投资者正在了解成为资本密集型旧经济工业企业的投资者是什么感觉。” “自由现金流不稳定，进入债务和股票市场对于融资至关重要。”

发行债务可能是一项深思熟虑的策略。它可以保留收购的流动性，同时在长期扩建融资方面带来灵活性。

Freedom Capital Markets 首席市场策略师 Jay Woods 正在评估每家公司的债务风险，而不是整个行业。例如，Nvidia 现金状况强劲，最新季度自由现金流跃升至 485 亿美元，高于去年同期的 261 亿美元。

伍兹在谈到英伟达时表示：“他们仍然拥有大量现金，所以我认为这并不是那么大的危险信号。” “这确实给了他们灵活性。”

——CNBC 的德鲁·特罗斯特 (Drew Troast) 贡献了报道。
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于财经市场信号，适合用于观察利率、汇率、股债商品、金融机构和宏观政策变化。
